title: How Do We Adapt to the Cold?

blanks.sentence: Listeners come from various locations worldwide, including extremely cold nations such as _____, where temperatures in winter can fall to -20°C
blanks.evidence: coming from all over the world, including some of the coldest countries on Earth – places like Finland, where winter temperatures drop to -20°C
blanks.section: Global cold environments and record temperatures

blanks.sentence: The coldest temperature documented on the planet was _____
blanks.evidence: the lowest temperature ever recorded was a very cold -89°C
blanks.section: Global cold environments and record temperatures

blanks.sentence: This extreme cold was recorded in _____
blanks.evidence: I'm going to guess b) Antarctica
blanks.section: Global cold environments and record temperatures

blanks.sentence: People's response to cold is influenced by multiple elements, including genetic makeup, cultural background, _____, and clothing choices
blanks.evidence: Our experience of cold depends on many things, including our genes, culture and place of birth
blanks.section: Factors affecting cold perception and clothing choices

blanks.sentence: Professor Gunhild Sætren resides on an icy island situated _____ inside the Arctic Circle
blanks.evidence: lives on the icy island of Svalbard, 800 miles inside the Arctic Circle
blanks.section: Factors affecting cold perception and clothing choices

blanks.sentence: Gender differences: males tend to be _____ than females
blanks.evidence: So, males often are more tolerant, perhaps, than females
blanks.section: Factors affecting cold perception and clothing choices

blanks.sentence: Experience: whether someone is accustomed to _____
blanks.evidence: Then it's of course what you're used to. Are you used to dealing with the cold?
blanks.section: Factors affecting cold perception and clothing choices

blanks.sentence: Herders experience _____ daily and have been doing so for hundreds of years
blanks.evidence: people who live in cold temperatures every day and have done for centuries
blanks.section: Research on reindeer herders in northern Finland

blanks.sentence: Their response is measured and contrasted with typical Finns from _____ of the nation
blanks.evidence: compares it to ordinary Finns from warmer parts of the country
blanks.section: Research on reindeer herders in northern Finland

blanks.sentence: Subjectively speaking, the herders cope with cold conditions _____
blanks.evidence: the reindeer herders, at least kind of mentally, handle the cold far better
blanks.section: Research on reindeer herders in northern Finland

blanks.evidence: They are far less likely to shiver
blanks.section: Research on reindeer herders in northern Finland

blanks.sentence: Cold adaptation may involve _____ inherited from parents to offspring
blanks.evidence: including genetic adaptations passed on from parents to children
blanks.section: Research on reindeer herders in northern Finland

blanks.sentence: The record-breaking temperature was measured at a research facility in _____
blanks.evidence: The lowest temperature ever recorded on Earth was at the Vostok Research Station in Antarctica
at the Vostok Research Station in Antarctica in 1983

blanks.sentence: A shiver is a trembling motion produced by _____ when experiencing cold or fear
blanks.evidence: a shiver is the shaking movement made by your muscles when you feel cold or afraid

multipleChoice.question: What is Becca's attitude towards cold weather compared to heat?
options: A. She prefers the cold and finds it refreshing
B. She prefers being too hot rather than too cold
C. She is indifferent to temperature extremes
D. She dislikes both heat and cold equally
multipleChoice.evidence: Becca: 'I'd rather be too hot and cool down than too cold and try to warm up.'

multipleChoice.question: What is the main purpose of Phil mentioning people in the UK wearing T-shirts while others wear warm clothes?
options: A. To explain how clothes protect us differently
B. To introduce the topic of varying cold tolerance
C. To highlight British fashion trends in winter
D. To criticize those who dress inappropriately for cold
multipleChoice.evidence: Phil: 'Why do people feel the cold so differently? That's what we'll be discussing…'

multipleChoice.question: What does Gunhild Sætren mean by 'first and foremost' when talking about gender differences in cold tolerance?
options: A. It is the most important factor to consider
B. It is one of the many minor factors
C. It is the least relevant consideration
D. It is an outdated idea no longer true
multipleChoice.evidence: Becca: 'first and foremost means more than anything else'

multipleChoice.question: Which factor besides gender does Gunhild mention affects how people feel the cold?
options: A. The clothes they wear daily
B. Whether they are used to cold environments
C. Their diet and nutrition
D. Their level of physical fitness
multipleChoice.evidence: Gunhild: 'Are you used to dealing with the cold?'

multipleChoice.question: Why does Phil say 'there is some debate about whether men or women are more tolerant of the cold'?
options: A. To show there is scientific consensus on gender differences
B. To provide proof men are definitely tougher
C. To introduce a disputed topic without firm conclusions
D. To emphasize that gender is irrelevant
multipleChoice.evidence: Phil: 'There is some debate about whether men or women are more tolerant…'

multipleChoice.question: What does Becca imply about people born in Arctic Svalbard compared with those from Brazil?
options: A. People from cold places feel less cold due to environmental and physical factors
B. People from Brazil are better at coping with the cold
C. Place of birth has no impact on cold tolerance
D. People from Svalbard dress poorly for the cold
multipleChoice.evidence: Phil: 'someone born in Arctic Svalbard would feel less cold than someone born in Brazil'

multipleChoice.question: What is the implied meaning of 'subjectively' in the context of reindeer herders handling cold?
options: A. Their tolerance is based on measured, objective facts
B. Their feelings are irrelevant to scientific study
C. Their experience of cold is based on personal perception rather than hard data
D. Their shivering indicates high tolerance objectively
multipleChoice.evidence: Phil: 'subjectively means based on personal experience… mentally handle the cold better'

multipleChoice.question: What does Dr Cara Ocobock mean when she says 'the more data we collect... the more confusing the picture gets'?
options: A. The effects of cold tolerance are very simple to understand
B. Their study has clear and predictable results
C. The findings about cold adaptation are complicated and not clear-cut
D. They have not yet started collecting any data
multipleChoice.evidence: Cara: 'the more confusing the picture gets'

multipleChoice.question: What can be inferred about reindeer herders' physical adaptation to extreme cold?
options: A. They have identical physical responses to ordinary Finns
B. They shiver more frequently than people from warmer regions
C. They are less likely to shiver, suggesting some physical adaptation
D. They do not need any protective clothing in Arctic conditions
multipleChoice.evidence: Cara: 'They are far less likely to shiver'

multipleChoice.question: According to the programme, what causes shivering in cold environments?
options: A. Shivering is an involuntary muscle movement to generate heat
B. Shivering is a voluntary exercise to warm up
C. Shivering is a sign of enjoying cold weather
D. Shivering only happens when a person is afraid
multipleChoice.evidence: Becca: 'a shiver is the shaking movement made by your muscles when you feel cold or afraid'
